name: WBC
text_chunk: White blood cell (WBC) count measures the total number of leukocytes in peripheral blood. It is a key indicator of immune system activity and is part of the complete blood count panel. Elevated WBC (leukocytosis) may indicate infection, inflammation, stress response, or hematologic malignancy. Low WBC (leukopenia) can signal bone marrow suppression, autoimmune conditions, or viral infections. Serial WBC trending helps monitor treatment response in infections and chemotherapy-induced neutropenia. The differential count provides additional detail about specific leukocyte populations.
clinical_significance: Elevated in infection, inflammation, stress, and leukemia; decreased in bone marrow failure, autoimmune conditions, and severe infections.

name: RBC
text_chunk: Red blood cell (RBC) count quantifies the number of erythrocytes per unit volume of blood. RBCs are responsible for oxygen transport via hemoglobin. Elevated RBC (polycythemia) may be primary (polycythemia vera, JAK2 mutation) or secondary to chronic hypoxia, high altitude, or erythropoietin-producing tumors. Low RBC indicates anemia from blood loss, hemolysis, or inadequate production. The RBC count is interpreted alongside hemoglobin, hematocrit, and red cell indices (MCV, MCH, MCHC) to classify anemia type.
clinical_significance: Elevated in polycythemia vera or chronic hypoxia; decreased in anemia from blood loss, nutritional deficiency, or bone marrow disease.

name: Hemoglobin
text_chunk: Hemoglobin (Hgb) is the oxygen-carrying protein within red blood cells. It is the most clinically useful measure for diagnosing and classifying anemia. Normal ranges differ by sex: males 13.5-17.5 g/dL, females 12.0-16.0 g/dL. Low hemoglobin triggers evaluation for iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, chronic disease, hemolysis, or bone marrow disorders. Critically low levels (<7 g/dL) may require transfusion. Hemoglobin is also used to assess blood loss severity and guide perioperative management.
clinical_significance: Primary marker for anemia diagnosis; critical values below 7 g/dL may require transfusion. Sex-specific reference ranges apply.

name: Hematocrit
text_chunk: Hematocrit (Hct) represents the percentage of blood volume occupied by red blood cells. It parallels hemoglobin in diagnosing anemia and polycythemia but is also affected by plasma volume changes. Dehydration falsely elevates hematocrit, while fluid overload lowers it. Normal ranges are sex-dependent: males 38.3-48.6%, females 35.5-44.9%. Hematocrit is used in calculating red cell indices and is a key parameter in blood viscosity assessment.
clinical_significance: Reflects red cell mass relative to plasma volume; elevated in dehydration and polycythemia, decreased in anemia and fluid overload.

name: MCV
text_chunk: Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) measures the average size of red blood cells in femtoliters. It is the primary index for morphologic classification of anemia. Microcytic anemia (MCV <80 fL) suggests iron deficiency, thalassemia, or chronic disease. Macrocytic anemia (MCV >100 fL) points to B12/folate deficiency, myelodysplasia, liver disease, or hypothyroidism. MCV is a component of the PhenoAge epigenetic clock, where higher values are associated with accelerated biological aging independent of anemia status.
clinical_significance: Classifies anemia as microcytic, normocytic, or macrocytic; elevated MCV is a PhenoAge aging biomarker independent of anemia.

name: MCH
text_chunk: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) measures the average mass of hemoglobin per red blood cell in picograms. MCH parallels MCV in anemia classification: low MCH indicates hypochromic cells (iron deficiency, thalassemia), while high MCH suggests macrocytic conditions. MCH is calculated as hemoglobin divided by RBC count. Unlike MCHC, MCH does not account for cell size, so it is most useful when interpreted alongside MCV and MCHC for comprehensive red cell characterization.
clinical_significance: Low MCH indicates hypochromic anemia (iron deficiency, thalassemia); elevated MCH parallels macrocytosis from B12 or folate deficiency.

name: MCHC
text_chunk: Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) measures the average concentration of hemoglobin within red blood cells. It is the most specific index for detecting hypochromia. Low MCHC (<32 g/dL) is characteristic of iron deficiency anemia and thalassemia. Elevated MCHC (>36 g/dL) is seen in hereditary spherocytosis, where cells are hyperchromic due to membrane loss. MCHC is relatively stable and less affected by reticulocytosis than MCV or MCH.
clinical_significance: Low MCHC is specific for iron deficiency; elevated MCHC suggests hereditary spherocytosis or cold agglutinins.

name: RDW
text_chunk: Red cell distribution width (RDW) measures the variation in red blood cell size (anisocytosis). Elevated RDW indicates a mixed population of cell sizes, commonly seen in iron deficiency anemia, B12/folate deficiency, and mixed nutritional deficiencies. RDW helps distinguish iron deficiency (elevated RDW) from thalassemia trait (normal RDW). Beyond hematology, elevated RDW is an independent predictor of cardiovascular mortality and all-cause mortality. It is a component of the PhenoAge biological aging clock.
clinical_significance: Elevated RDW differentiates iron deficiency from thalassemia trait; independently predicts cardiovascular and all-cause mortality.

name: Platelet Count
text_chunk: Platelet count measures the number of thrombocytes per microliter of blood. Platelets are essential for primary hemostasis and clot formation. Thrombocytopenia (<150,000/uL) may result from decreased production (marrow failure, chemotherapy), increased destruction (ITP, TTP, HIT, DIC), or sequestration (splenomegaly). Thrombocytosis (>400,000/uL) can be reactive (infection, inflammation, iron deficiency) or clonal (essential thrombocythemia, other myeloproliferative neoplasms).
clinical_significance: Low platelets increase bleeding risk; elevated platelets may be reactive or indicate myeloproliferative neoplasm.

name: MPV
text_chunk: Mean platelet volume (MPV) measures the average size of platelets. Larger platelets are younger and more metabolically active. Elevated MPV with low platelet count suggests peripheral destruction (ITP), where the marrow compensates by releasing larger platelets. Low MPV with thrombocytopenia suggests marrow failure. MPV is emerging as a biomarker for cardiovascular risk, as larger platelets are more prothrombotic.
clinical_significance: Elevated MPV suggests platelet destruction or cardiovascular risk; low MPV with thrombocytopenia indicates marrow suppression.

name: Neutrophils
text_chunk: Neutrophils (absolute neutrophil count, ANC) are the most abundant white blood cells and the primary defense against bacterial and fungal infections. Neutrophilia occurs in bacterial infection, inflammation, stress, corticosteroid use, and myeloproliferative disorders. Neutropenia (ANC <1500/uL) increases infection risk, with severe neutropenia (<500/uL) requiring urgent evaluation. The neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) is an emerging inflammatory and prognostic biomarker.
clinical_significance: Elevated in bacterial infection and inflammation; neutropenia below 500/uL constitutes a medical emergency with high infection risk.

name: Lymphocytes
text_chunk: Lymphocytes include T cells, B cells, and natural killer (NK) cells, forming the adaptive immune system. Lymphocytosis occurs in viral infections (EBV, CMV, HIV), chronic lymphocytic leukemia, and pertussis. Lymphopenia is seen in HIV/AIDS, corticosteroid therapy, autoimmune disease, and severe sepsis. Absolute lymphocyte count is prognostic in cancer and critical illness.
clinical_significance: Elevated in viral infections and lymphoproliferative disorders; lymphopenia is prognostic in critical illness and HIV.

name: Lymphocyte %
text_chunk: Lymphocyte percentage represents the proportion of lymphocytes among total white blood cells. It is a component of the PhenoAge epigenetic clock algorithm developed by Levine et al. (2018). Lower lymphocyte percentage is associated with accelerated biological aging, immune senescence, and increased all-cause mortality. In the PhenoAge model, lymphocyte percentage has a negative coefficient, meaning higher values are protective. This parameter reflects overall immune competence and thymic output capacity.
clinical_significance: PhenoAge clock component; lower lymphocyte percentage indicates immune senescence and accelerated biological aging.

name: Monocytes
text_chunk: Monocytes are large phagocytic white blood cells that differentiate into macrophages and dendritic cells in tissues. Monocytosis occurs in chronic infections (tuberculosis, endocarditis), autoimmune diseases, inflammatory bowel disease, and certain malignancies (CMML). Monocytes play critical roles in innate immunity, antigen presentation, and tissue repair. The monocyte-to-HDL ratio is emerging as a cardiovascular risk biomarker.
clinical_significance: Elevated in chronic infections, autoimmune disease, and myeloproliferative disorders; monocyte-to-HDL ratio indicates cardiovascular risk.

name: Eosinophils
text_chunk: Eosinophils are granulocytes involved in parasitic defense, allergic reactions, and immune modulation. Eosinophilia (>500/uL) is classified as mild (500-1500), moderate (1500-5000), or severe (>5000). Common causes include allergic diseases (asthma, eczema), parasitic infections, drug hypersensitivity, and eosinophilic gastrointestinal disorders. Eosinophil counts guide anti-IL-5 biologic therapy selection in severe asthma.
clinical_significance: Elevated in allergic conditions, parasitic infections, and eosinophilic disorders; guides biologic therapy selection in asthma.

name: Basophils
text_chunk: Basophils are the rarest circulating granulocytes, containing histamine and heparin granules. They mediate immediate hypersensitivity reactions and play roles in allergic inflammation. Basophilia (>0.1 x10^3/uL) is uncommon and may indicate chronic myeloid leukemia, myeloproliferative neoplasms, hypothyroidism, or chronic inflammatory conditions. Persistent basophilia warrants BCR-ABL testing.
clinical_significance: Basophilia may indicate myeloproliferative neoplasm, particularly CML; basophil activation testing aids allergy diagnosis.

name: Neutrophil-Lymphocyte Ratio
text_chunk: The neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) is calculated by dividing the absolute neutrophil count by the absolute lymphocyte count. NLR is an integrated marker of systemic inflammation and immune balance. Elevated NLR (>3.0) is associated with worse prognosis in cardiovascular disease, cancer, sepsis, and COVID-19. NLR above 6.0 indicates significant systemic inflammation. It is inexpensive, widely available, and increasingly used as a prognostic biomarker.
clinical_significance: Elevated NLR predicts worse outcomes in cardiovascular disease, cancer, and critical illness; a low-cost inflammation marker.

name: Reticulocyte Count
text_chunk: Reticulocytes are immature red blood cells released from bone marrow, containing residual RNA. The reticulocyte count reflects marrow erythropoietic activity and is essential for classifying anemia. Elevated reticulocytes (>2.5%) indicate appropriate marrow response to anemia from blood loss or hemolysis. Low reticulocytes in anemia suggest marrow failure, nutritional deficiency, or anemia of chronic disease. The reticulocyte production index corrects for anemia severity.
clinical_significance: Elevated reticulocytes indicate marrow response to blood loss or hemolysis; low reticulocytes in anemia suggest production failure.

name: Glucose (fasting)
text_chunk: Fasting blood glucose measures blood sugar after at least 8 hours without caloric intake. It is the primary screening test for diabetes mellitus. Normal fasting glucose is 70-99 mg/dL; impaired fasting glucose (pre-diabetes) is 100-125 mg/dL; diabetes is diagnosed at 126 mg/dL or above on two occasions. Fasting glucose is a component of the PhenoAge epigenetic clock, where higher levels correlate with accelerated biological aging. Hypoglycemia (<70 mg/dL) may indicate insulinoma, adrenal insufficiency, or medication effects.
clinical_significance: Primary diabetes screening marker; fasting glucose 100-125 mg/dL indicates pre-diabetes. PhenoAge clock component.

name: BUN
text_chunk: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) measures the amount of nitrogen in urea, a waste product of protein metabolism cleared by the kidneys. Elevated BUN occurs in renal impairment, dehydration, high protein diet, gastrointestinal bleeding, and catabolic states. Low BUN may indicate liver disease, malnutrition, or overhydration. BUN is interpreted alongside creatinine; a BUN/creatinine ratio above 20:1 suggests prerenal azotemia.
clinical_significance: Elevated in renal impairment and dehydration; BUN/creatinine ratio helps distinguish prerenal from intrinsic renal disease.

name: Creatinine
text_chunk: Serum creatinine is produced from creatine phosphate in skeletal muscle and is filtered by the kidneys at a relatively constant rate. It is the most widely used marker of kidney function and is used to calculate estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR). Normal ranges are sex-dependent: males 0.7-1.3 mg/dL, females 0.6-1.1 mg/dL. Creatinine is a PhenoAge clock component, where elevated levels indicate accelerated biological aging. Creatinine is affected by muscle mass, diet, and certain medications.
clinical_significance: Primary kidney function marker used to calculate eGFR; PhenoAge clock component. Affected by muscle mass and diet.

name: BUN/Creatinine Ratio
text_chunk: The BUN/creatinine ratio helps differentiate causes of elevated BUN. A ratio above 20:1 suggests prerenal azotemia from dehydration, congestive heart failure, or gastrointestinal bleeding. A ratio of 10-20:1 with both BUN and creatinine elevated indicates intrinsic renal disease. A ratio below 10:1 may indicate liver disease, low protein diet, or rhabdomyolysis.
clinical_significance: Ratio above 20:1 suggests prerenal azotemia; below 10:1 may indicate liver disease or rhabdomyolysis.

name: Sodium
text_chunk: Serum sodium is the primary extracellular cation and the major determinant of plasma osmolality and fluid balance. Hyponatremia (<136 mEq/L) is the most common electrolyte disorder in hospitalized patients, caused by SIADH, diuretics, heart failure, cirrhosis, or adrenal insufficiency. Severe hyponatremia (<120 mEq/L) causes cerebral edema and seizures. Hypernatremia (>145 mEq/L) indicates water deficit. Rapid correction of either disorder can cause osmotic demyelination.
clinical_significance: Most common electrolyte disorder; severe hyponatremia or hypernatremia is life-threatening. Correction rate must be carefully controlled.

name: Potassium
text_chunk: Serum potassium is the primary intracellular cation, critical for maintaining resting membrane potential and cardiac rhythm. Hypokalemia (<3.5 mEq/L) causes muscle weakness, arrhythmias, and rhabdomyolysis. Hyperkalemia (>5.0 mEq/L) causes peaked T waves and can lead to fatal ventricular fibrillation. Causes include renal failure, ACE inhibitors, and metabolic acidosis. Hemolyzed specimens falsely elevate results.
clinical_significance: Critical electrolyte for cardiac function; both hypo- and hyperkalemia can cause fatal arrhythmias. Check for hemolysis.

name: Chloride
text_chunk: Serum chloride is the major extracellular anion, working with sodium to maintain osmolality and acid-base balance. Chloride is essential for calculating the anion gap (Na - Cl - HCO3), which helps classify metabolic acidosis. Hyperchloremia occurs in normal anion gap metabolic acidosis, dehydration, and excessive saline administration. Hypochloremia is seen in metabolic alkalosis, vomiting, and SIADH.
clinical_significance: Essential for anion gap calculation and acid-base diagnosis; hyperchloremic acidosis from saline overuse is common in ICU settings.

name: CO2/Bicarbonate
text_chunk: Serum CO2 (total CO2) predominantly reflects bicarbonate (HCO3-), the major blood buffer. Low CO2 indicates metabolic acidosis (diabetic ketoacidosis, lactic acidosis, renal tubular acidosis, diarrhea) or respiratory compensation for alkalosis. Elevated CO2 suggests metabolic alkalosis (vomiting, diuretics) or respiratory compensation for acidosis. Chronic low bicarbonate in CKD patients accelerates bone loss and muscle wasting.
clinical_significance: Reflects acid-base status; low CO2 indicates metabolic acidosis, elevated CO2 suggests metabolic alkalosis. Interpret with anion gap.

name: Calcium
text_chunk: Serum calcium measures total calcium (bound + ionized). Approximately 40% is protein-bound (mainly albumin), so calcium must be corrected for albumin: corrected Ca = measured Ca + 0.8 x (4.0 - albumin). Hypercalcemia (>10.5 mg/dL) is most commonly caused by primary hyperparathyroidism and malignancy. Hypocalcemia (<8.5 mg/dL) causes neuromuscular irritability, tetany, and QT prolongation.
clinical_significance: Must be corrected for albumin; hypercalcemia suggests hyperparathyroidism or malignancy, hypocalcemia indicates vitamin D deficiency or hypoparathyroidism.

name: Total Protein
text_chunk: Total serum protein is the sum of albumin and globulins. Elevated total protein may indicate chronic inflammation, multiple myeloma, chronic infection, or dehydration. Low total protein suggests malnutrition, nephrotic syndrome, liver disease, or protein-losing enteropathy. Serum protein electrophoresis is ordered when monoclonal gammopathy is suspected based on elevated total protein with a high globulin fraction.
clinical_significance: Elevated in myeloma and chronic inflammation; decreased in malnutrition, liver disease, and nephrotic syndrome.

name: Albumin
text_chunk: Serum albumin is the most abundant plasma protein, synthesized by the liver. It maintains oncotic pressure, transports hormones, drugs, and fatty acids, and serves as a nutritional and prognostic marker. Low albumin (<3.5 g/dL) indicates chronic illness, liver disease, nephrotic syndrome, malnutrition, or systemic inflammation. Albumin is a PhenoAge epigenetic clock component with a negative coefficient, meaning lower albumin strongly predicts accelerated biological aging and mortality.
clinical_significance: PhenoAge clock component; low albumin is a strong predictor of mortality and indicates chronic disease, liver dysfunction, or malnutrition.

name: Globulin
text_chunk: Serum globulin is calculated as total protein minus albumin and includes alpha, beta, and gamma globulins (immunoglobulins). Elevated globulin suggests chronic inflammation, autoimmune disease, chronic infection, or plasma cell neoplasm. Low globulin may indicate immunodeficiency or nephrotic syndrome. Serum protein electrophoresis differentiates polyclonal from monoclonal gammopathy.
clinical_significance: Elevated in chronic inflammation, autoimmune disease, and multiple myeloma; low values suggest immunodeficiency.

name: A/G Ratio
text_chunk: The albumin-to-globulin (A/G) ratio is calculated by dividing albumin by globulin. A low A/G ratio (<1.0) suggests overproduction of globulins (multiple myeloma, autoimmune disease) or underproduction of albumin (liver disease, malnutrition). Reversal (ratio <1.0) warrants serum protein electrophoresis, immunofixation, and further workup for monoclonal gammopathy.
clinical_significance: Reversal (ratio <1.0) warrants myeloma workup; reflects balance between liver synthetic function and immune activation.

name: eGFR
text_chunk: Estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) is calculated from serum creatinine using the CKD-EPI equation. eGFR stages: normal (>90), mild decrease (60-89), moderate CKD (30-59), severe CKD (15-29), kidney failure (<15). eGFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m2 for 3+ months defines chronic kidney disease. Cystatin C-based eGFR is more accurate in extremes of muscle mass. eGFR guides drug dosing and nephrology referral timing.
clinical_significance: Primary CKD staging marker; eGFR below 60 for 3+ months defines CKD. Guides drug dosing and nephrology referral.

name: ALT
text_chunk: Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) is a liver-specific enzyme that catalyzes transamination in hepatocytes. Elevated ALT indicates hepatocellular injury and is more specific for liver disease than AST. Common causes include viral hepatitis, NAFLD/NASH, drug-induced liver injury (acetaminophen), and autoimmune hepatitis. ALT above 10x ULN suggests acute hepatitis. The PNPLA3 rs738409 GG genotype doubles ALT levels in NAFLD.
clinical_significance: Most specific marker for hepatocellular injury; mild elevations common in NAFLD. Genotype at PNPLA3 significantly modifies ALT levels.

name: AST
text_chunk: Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) is found in liver, heart, muscle, kidney, and brain. Elevated AST is less specific for liver disease than ALT. AST/ALT ratio above 2:1 suggests alcoholic liver disease (De Ritis ratio). AST above 10x ULN indicates acute hepatitis, ischemic hepatitis, or drug toxicity. Isolated AST elevation without ALT elevation suggests cardiac or skeletal muscle source.
clinical_significance: Less liver-specific than ALT; AST/ALT ratio above 2:1 suggests alcoholic hepatitis. Elevated alone may indicate cardiac or muscle injury.

name: ALP
text_chunk: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is present in liver, bone, intestine, kidney, and placenta. Elevated ALP with elevated GGT confirms hepatobiliary origin (cholestasis, biliary obstruction). Elevated ALP with normal GGT suggests bone origin (Paget disease, osteomalacia). ALP is a component of the PhenoAge epigenetic clock, where elevated values are associated with accelerated biological aging and increased mortality risk.
clinical_significance: PhenoAge clock component; elevated with GGT confirms cholestatic liver disease. Isolated elevation suggests bone disease.

name: Total Bilirubin
text_chunk: Total bilirubin is the sum of unconjugated (indirect) and conjugated (direct) bilirubin. Elevated total bilirubin causes jaundice when above 2.5-3.0 mg/dL. Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia results from hemolysis or Gilbert syndrome (UGT1A1 polymorphism, affecting 5-10% of population). Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia indicates hepatocellular disease or biliary obstruction. Total bilirubin is part of the MELD score.
clinical_significance: Elevated in hemolysis, liver disease, and biliary obstruction; Gilbert syndrome is a benign cause. Component of MELD score.

name: Direct Bilirubin
text_chunk: Direct (conjugated) bilirubin measures the water-soluble fraction processed by the liver. Elevated direct bilirubin indicates impaired biliary excretion from hepatocellular disease, intrahepatic cholestasis, or extrahepatic biliary obstruction. Causes include gallstones, pancreatic cancer, primary biliary cholangitis, and drug-induced cholestasis.
clinical_significance: Elevated in biliary obstruction and cholestatic liver disease; urgent imaging needed when combined with obstructive symptoms.

name: GGT
text_chunk: Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) is a biliary enzyme used to confirm the hepatic origin of elevated ALP. GGT is highly sensitive for hepatobiliary disease but lacks specificity, as it is induced by alcohol, many medications, and metabolic syndrome. Elevated GGT is an independent predictor of cardiovascular mortality and type 2 diabetes. Serial GGT monitoring is used to assess alcohol abstinence.
clinical_significance: Confirms hepatic origin of elevated ALP; independent cardiovascular risk predictor. Sensitive marker for alcohol use.

name: FIB-4 Index
text_chunk: The FIB-4 index is a non-invasive fibrosis score calculated from age, AST, ALT, and platelet count: (Age x AST) / (Platelets x sqrt(ALT)). FIB-4 below 1.3 has a 90% negative predictive value for excluding advanced fibrosis. FIB-4 above 2.67 has a 97% specificity for confirming advanced fibrosis. FIB-4 is recommended by AASLD and EASL as the first-line non-invasive fibrosis assessment in NAFLD.
clinical_significance: Non-invasive fibrosis score; below 1.3 excludes advanced fibrosis, above 2.67 confirms it. First-line NAFLD fibrosis assessment.

name: Total Cholesterol
text_chunk: Total cholesterol measures the sum of LDL, HDL, and VLDL cholesterol. Desirable is below 200 mg/dL; borderline high 200-239; high is 240+ mg/dL. Total cholesterol alone is insufficient for cardiovascular risk assessment because it does not distinguish atherogenic from protective fractions. It is a component of the Framingham Risk Score. Familial hypercholesterolemia should be suspected when total cholesterol exceeds 310 mg/dL.
clinical_significance: Initial cardiovascular screening marker; must be interpreted with LDL and HDL fractions for accurate risk assessment.

name: LDL
text_chunk: Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol is the primary atherogenic lipoprotein and the primary target of cardiovascular risk reduction therapy. Optimal LDL is below 100 mg/dL; below 70 for high-risk patients; below 55 for very high-risk per ESC guidelines. LDL is strongly modified by APOE genotype, PCSK9 variants, and LDLR mutations. Statin therapy reduces LDL 30-50%; PCSK9 inhibitors provide additional 50-60% reduction.
clinical_significance: Primary target for cardiovascular risk reduction; optimal below 100 mg/dL. Strongly modified by APOE and PCSK9 genotype.

name: HDL
text_chunk: High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol mediates reverse cholesterol transport. HDL below 40 mg/dL in men and below 50 in women is an independent cardiovascular risk factor. Mendelian randomization studies show genetically elevated HDL does not reduce cardiovascular events. HDL functionality (cholesterol efflux capacity) may matter more than absolute levels. Low HDL is part of metabolic syndrome criteria.
clinical_significance: Low HDL is a cardiovascular risk factor; raising HDL pharmacologically has not reduced events. Functionality matters more than level.

name: Triglycerides
text_chunk: Triglycerides are the main form of stored fat and a major energy source. Desirable levels below 150 mg/dL; very high 500+ mg/dL carries pancreatitis risk. Elevated triglycerides are associated with metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Significantly affected by the GCKR rs1260326 variant. Omega-3 fatty acids and fibrates are primary therapies.
clinical_significance: Elevated triglycerides indicate metabolic syndrome and pancreatitis risk above 500 mg/dL; modified by GCKR genotype.

name: VLDL
text_chunk: Very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) cholesterol is a triglyceride-rich lipoprotein produced by the liver. VLDL is typically estimated as triglycerides divided by 5 (Friedewald equation, valid when TG <400 mg/dL). Elevated VLDL is atherogenic and contributes to residual cardiovascular risk beyond LDL. VLDL remnants promote foam cell formation in arterial walls.
clinical_significance: Atherogenic triglyceride-rich lipoprotein; elevated in insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome.

name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
text_chunk: Non-HDL cholesterol (total cholesterol minus HDL) captures all atherogenic lipoproteins (LDL, VLDL, IDL, Lp(a)). It is a better predictor of cardiovascular risk than LDL alone, especially with high triglycerides. Non-HDL goals are 30 mg/dL above corresponding LDL goals. No fasting required. Recommended as secondary lipid target by AHA/ACC guidelines.
clinical_significance: Better cardiovascular predictor than LDL in hypertriglyceridemia; captures all atherogenic particles. No fasting required.

name: Total/HDL Ratio
text_chunk: The total cholesterol to HDL ratio is a simple cardiovascular risk metric. Below 5.0 is desirable; optimal is below 3.5. It reflects balance between atherogenic and antiatherogenic lipoproteins and is a component of the Framingham Risk Score. Lifestyle modifications (exercise, weight loss, Mediterranean diet) improve this ratio effectively.
clinical_significance: Cardiovascular risk ratio; optimal below 3.5. Component of Framingham Risk Score.

name: ApoB
text_chunk: Apolipoprotein B (ApoB) is the structural protein on all atherogenic lipoprotein particles. Since each particle carries one ApoB molecule, ApoB directly measures atherogenic particle number. ApoB is superior to LDL-C for cardiovascular risk assessment when particle number and cholesterol content are discordant. Optimal ApoB is below 90 mg/dL; below 65 for high-risk patients. Does not require fasting.
clinical_significance: Directly measures atherogenic particle number; superior to LDL for cardiovascular risk. Optimal below 90 mg/dL.

name: Lp(a)
text_chunk: Lipoprotein(a) is a genetically determined LDL-like particle with an additional apolipoprotein(a) chain. Levels above 50 mg/dL (125 nmol/L) confer significant cardiovascular and aortic stenosis risk. Lp(a) is 80-90% genetically determined by the LPA gene. Not significantly affected by diet, exercise, or statins. PCSK9 inhibitors lower Lp(a) by 20-30%. Lifetime testing once is sufficient.
clinical_significance: Genetically determined cardiovascular risk factor; above 50 mg/dL significantly increases risk. Test once in a lifetime.

name: LDL Particle Number
text_chunk: LDL particle number (LDL-P) measured by NMR lipoprofile directly quantifies LDL particles. LDL-P can be discordant with LDL-C in up to 25% of patients, particularly those with metabolic syndrome who have small dense LDL. When discordant, LDL-P better predicts cardiovascular events. Optimal LDL-P is below 1000 nmol/L.
clinical_significance: Directly measures LDL particle count; superior to LDL-C when discordant. Identifies residual risk in metabolic syndrome.

name: HbA1c
text_chunk: Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) reflects average blood glucose over the preceding 2-3 months by measuring the percentage of glycated hemoglobin. Normal is below 5.7%; pre-diabetes is 5.7-6.4%; diabetes is diagnosed at 6.5% or above. Modified by TCF7L2 genotype. Conditions that alter red cell lifespan can falsify results. Targets are individualized: below 7% for most diabetics, below 8% for elderly.
clinical_significance: Primary diabetes monitoring marker; 5.7-6.4% indicates pre-diabetes, above 6.5% diagnoses diabetes. Modified by TCF7L2 genotype.

name: Fasting Insulin
text_chunk: Fasting insulin measures circulating insulin after an overnight fast. It is an early marker of insulin resistance, often elevated years before glucose abnormalities appear. Optimal is below 10 uIU/mL; above 15 suggests significant insulin resistance. Combined with fasting glucose it calculates HOMA-IR. Elevated fasting insulin with normal glucose indicates compensated insulin resistance.
clinical_significance: Early marker of insulin resistance; optimal below 10 uIU/mL. Elevated with normal glucose indicates compensated resistance.

name: HOMA-IR
text_chunk: The Homeostatic Model Assessment of Insulin Resistance (HOMA-IR) is calculated as (fasting insulin x fasting glucose) / 405. Below 1.0 is optimal; 1.0-1.9 is early insulin resistance; 2.0-2.9 is significant; above 3.0 indicates severe insulin resistance. HOMA-IR detects metabolic dysfunction 10-15 years before diabetes diagnosis and is an independent predictor of cardiovascular events.
clinical_significance: Gold standard surrogate for insulin resistance; above 2.0 indicates significant resistance. Predicts diabetes 10-15 years before diagnosis.

name: C-Peptide
text_chunk: C-Peptide is co-secreted with insulin in equimolar amounts but is not extracted by the liver, making it a reliable marker of endogenous insulin production. Essential for distinguishing type 1 (low/absent) from type 2 (normal/elevated) diabetes and for detecting factitious insulin use. Falling C-Peptide indicates beta-cell failure and the need for insulin therapy.
clinical_significance: Distinguishes type 1 from type 2 diabetes; measures endogenous insulin production. Falling levels indicate beta-cell failure.

name: Fructosamine
text_chunk: Fructosamine measures glycated serum proteins (primarily albumin) and reflects average blood glucose over the preceding 2-3 weeks. Alternative to HbA1c when conditions alter red cell lifespan (hemolytic anemia, hemoglobin variants, recent transfusion, pregnancy). Also useful for monitoring rapid glycemic changes after medication adjustments. Affected by albumin levels.
clinical_significance: Alternative to HbA1c for 2-3 week glycemic control; use when hemoglobin variants or altered red cell lifespan affect HbA1c.

name: Adiponectin
text_chunk: Adiponectin is an adipokine with insulin-sensitizing, anti-inflammatory, and antiatherogenic properties. Unlike most adipokines, adiponectin decreases with increasing adiposity. Low adiponectin is associated with insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome, NAFLD, and cardiovascular disease. Inversely correlated with visceral fat mass. Thiazolidinediones significantly raise adiponectin levels.
clinical_significance: Insulin-sensitizing adipokine; low levels predict diabetes, metabolic syndrome, and cardiovascular disease independent of BMI.

name: Leptin
text_chunk: Leptin is a satiety hormone produced by adipose tissue that signals energy sufficiency to the hypothalamus. Levels correlate directly with body fat mass. Obesity causes leptin resistance. Leptin is a GrimAge epigenetic clock component, where elevated levels are associated with accelerated biological aging. Low leptin indicates lipodystrophy or severe caloric restriction.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component; elevated in obesity (leptin resistance), low in lipodystrophy. Reflects adipose tissue mass and metabolic aging.

name: IGF-1
text_chunk: Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) is produced primarily by the liver in response to growth hormone. Primary screening test for acromegaly (elevated) and GH deficiency (low). Age-specific reference ranges are essential. Both very low and very high IGF-1 are associated with increased mortality (U-shaped relationship). Plays roles in cancer biology and longevity pathways.
clinical_significance: Primary screening marker for acromegaly and GH deficiency; U-shaped mortality relationship. Age-specific ranges required.

name: TSH
text_chunk: Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) is the most sensitive screening test for thyroid dysfunction. Inversely related to thyroid hormone levels via negative feedback. Elevated TSH indicates hypothyroidism; suppressed TSH indicates hyperthyroidism. Subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 4.0-10.0 with normal FT4) affects 5-10% of adults. Optimal TSH is 0.5-2.5 mIU/L. Biotin supplements can interfere with TSH assays.
clinical_significance: Most sensitive thyroid screening test; elevated indicates hypothyroidism, suppressed indicates hyperthyroidism. Optimal 0.5-2.5 mIU/L.

name: Free T4
text_chunk: Free thyroxine (FT4) measures the unbound, biologically active fraction of T4. Combined with TSH it provides primary thyroid function assessment. Low FT4 with elevated TSH confirms primary hypothyroidism; low FT4 with low/normal TSH suggests secondary hypothyroidism. Not affected by binding protein changes that alter total T4.
clinical_significance: Measures biologically active thyroid hormone; combined with TSH for complete thyroid assessment.

name: Free T3
text_chunk: Free triiodothyronine (FT3) is the most metabolically active thyroid hormone, primarily produced by peripheral conversion of T4 to T3 by deiodinase enzymes. Essential for diagnosing T3 thyrotoxicosis. Low FT3 with normal T4 (euthyroid sick syndrome) occurs in critical illness. The DIO2 rs225014 variant impairs T4-to-T3 conversion, causing some patients to respond better to combination T4/T3 therapy.
clinical_significance: Most active thyroid hormone; diagnoses T3 thyrotoxicosis. DIO2 genotype affects T4-to-T3 conversion and therapy response.

name: Total T4
text_chunk: Total thyroxine (T4) measures both protein-bound and free T4. Affected by thyroid-binding globulin levels, which increase in pregnancy, estrogen therapy, and liver disease. Free T4 is preferred for clinical decision-making. Total T4 remains useful in monitoring amiodarone-induced thyroid dysfunction.
clinical_significance: Affected by binding protein changes; free T4 is preferred. Useful for monitoring amiodarone-related thyroid dysfunction.

name: Reverse T3
text_chunk: Reverse T3 (rT3) is the metabolically inactive isomer of T3, produced by type 3 deiodinase from T4. Elevated rT3 occurs in nonthyroidal illness (sick euthyroid syndrome), where the body shunts T4 metabolism toward inactive rT3. Characteristic of critical illness, caloric restriction, chronic stress, and selenium deficiency.
clinical_significance: Elevated in nonthyroidal illness (sick euthyroid syndrome); reflects metabolic conservation.

name: Thyroid Peroxidase Antibodies
text_chunk: Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies are autoantibodies present in 90% of Hashimoto thyroiditis and 75% of Graves disease patients. With subclinical hypothyroidism, they predict progression to overt hypothyroidism at approximately 4% per year. Found in 10-15% of the general population. Associated with thyroid cancer risk and pregnancy complications.
clinical_significance: Diagnostic for autoimmune thyroid disease; positive with subclinical hypothyroidism predicts 4%/year progression to overt hypothyroidism.

name: Serum Iron
text_chunk: Serum iron measures circulating iron bound to transferrin. Has significant diurnal variation (highest in morning) and is affected by recent iron intake. Low serum iron occurs in iron deficiency, chronic inflammation, and blood loss. Elevated in hemochromatosis, transfusional siderosis, and liver disease. Best interpreted alongside ferritin, TIBC, and transferrin saturation.
clinical_significance: Measures circulating iron; significant diurnal variation limits isolated utility. Interpret with ferritin and transferrin saturation.

name: Ferritin
text_chunk: Ferritin is the primary intracellular iron storage protein and most sensitive single test for iron deficiency. Below 30 ng/mL is diagnostic (sensitivity 92%, specificity 98%). Also an acute phase reactant elevated in inflammation. In inflammatory states, below 100 ng/mL with TSAT below 20% suggests iron deficiency. Above 1000 ng/mL warrants HFE genotyping.
clinical_significance: Most sensitive iron deficiency marker; below 30 ng/mL diagnostic. Also an acute phase reactant elevated in inflammation.

name: TIBC
text_chunk: Total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) measures the maximum amount of iron transferrin can bind, reflecting transferrin concentration. Elevated in iron deficiency; low in iron overload, chronic inflammation, and liver disease. Used to calculate transferrin saturation (TSAT = serum iron / TIBC x 100).
clinical_significance: Reflects transferrin levels; elevated in iron deficiency, decreased in iron overload and inflammation.

name: Transferrin Saturation
text_chunk: Transferrin saturation (TSAT) is calculated as (serum iron / TIBC) x 100. Below 20% indicates iron deficiency and is the threshold for IV iron therapy in heart failure and CKD. Above 45% suggests iron overload and triggers HFE genotyping for hemochromatosis. In HFE C282Y homozygotes, elevated TSAT is often the earliest finding, preceding ferritin elevation by years.
clinical_significance: Below 20% indicates iron deficiency; above 45% triggers hemochromatosis evaluation. Earliest marker of HFE-related iron overload.

name: Soluble Transferrin Receptor
text_chunk: Soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR) is shed from cell surfaces in proportion to cellular iron demand. Unlike ferritin, sTfR is not affected by inflammation, making it valuable for diagnosing iron deficiency with concurrent chronic disease. The sTfR/ferritin ratio is the most accurate method for distinguishing iron deficiency anemia from anemia of chronic disease.
clinical_significance: Not affected by inflammation; best test for iron deficiency in chronic disease. sTfR/ferritin ratio distinguishes IDA from ACD.

name: hs-CRP
text_chunk: High-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) measures low-level systemic inflammation and is an independent cardiovascular risk predictor. Risk categories: low <1.0, moderate 1.0-3.0, high >3.0 mg/L. JUPITER trial demonstrated statin benefit with elevated hs-CRP and normal LDL. PhenoAge epigenetic clock component where elevated levels strongly predict accelerated biological aging and mortality.
clinical_significance: PhenoAge clock component and cardiovascular risk predictor; above 3.0 mg/L indicates high risk.

name: ESR
text_chunk: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is a nonspecific inflammation marker. ESR above 100 mm/hr strongly suggests serious pathology: malignancy, infection (endocarditis, osteomyelitis), autoimmune disease (giant cell arteritis), or myeloma. Primary monitoring marker for giant cell arteritis and polymyalgia rheumatica.
clinical_significance: Nonspecific inflammation marker; ESR above 100 mm/hr suggests serious pathology. Primary monitoring marker for giant cell arteritis.

name: IL-6
text_chunk: Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is a pleiotropic pro-inflammatory cytokine that stimulates hepatic CRP production and drives the acute phase response. Elevated in aging (inflammaging), obesity, cardiovascular disease, and cancer. Levels rise exponentially with age. Tocilizumab (anti-IL-6R antibody) treats rheumatoid arthritis and cytokine release syndrome.
clinical_significance: Key pro-inflammatory cytokine driving CRP production; elevated in aging, obesity, and severe inflammation.

name: TNF-alpha
text_chunk: Tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-alpha) is a master pro-inflammatory cytokine produced primarily by macrophages. Elevated in rheumatoid arthritis, IBD, psoriasis, and obesity. Anti-TNF therapies (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept) revolutionized autoimmune disease treatment. Chronic low-grade elevation contributes to inflammaging, insulin resistance, and sarcopenia.
clinical_significance: Master inflammatory cytokine; chronic elevation contributes to inflammaging and insulin resistance.

name: Fibrinogen
text_chunk: Fibrinogen is a coagulation factor (Factor I) and acute phase reactant. Elevated fibrinogen is an independent cardiovascular risk factor, increasing blood viscosity and platelet aggregation. Low fibrinogen (<100 mg/dL) indicates DIC, liver failure, or congenital afibrinogenemia. Above 700 mg/dL significantly increases thrombotic risk.
clinical_significance: Acute phase reactant and coagulation factor; elevated levels increase cardiovascular and thrombotic risk. Low levels suggest DIC.

name: Homocysteine
text_chunk: Homocysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid in methionine metabolism. Elevated homocysteine (>15 umol/L) is an independent cardiovascular risk factor associated with VTE, cognitive decline, and osteoporosis. MTHFR C677T (rs1801133) is the most common genetic cause, reducing enzyme activity 30% (het) to 70% (hom). B-vitamin supplementation lowers levels but has not reduced CV events in trials.
clinical_significance: Cardiovascular risk factor modified by MTHFR genotype; elevated in B12, folate, or B6 deficiency.

name: GDF-15
text_chunk: Growth differentiation factor 15 (GDF-15) is a stress-responsive TGF-beta superfamily cytokine and one of the strongest biomarkers of biological aging and all-cause mortality. Core component of the GrimAge epigenetic clock. Levels increase exponentially with age, chronic disease, cancer, heart failure, and mitochondrial dysfunction. Also mediates cancer cachexia.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component and strongest aging biomarker; elevated in cancer, heart failure, and mitochondrial dysfunction.

name: PAI-1
text_chunk: Plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 (PAI-1) is the primary inhibitor of tissue plasminogen activator, regulating fibrinolysis. Elevated PAI-1 promotes prothrombotic state and is associated with cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and obesity. GrimAge epigenetic clock component. The 4G/5G SERPINE1 promoter polymorphism affects expression levels.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component; promotes prothrombotic state. Elevated in metabolic syndrome and visceral obesity.

name: Vitamin D (25-OH)
text_chunk: 25-hydroxyvitamin D is the primary circulating form and best marker of vitamin D status. Deficiency below 20 ng/mL; insufficiency 20-29; sufficiency 30-100 ng/mL. Affects 1 billion people globally. Associated with osteoporosis, autoimmune disease, cardiovascular disease, and cancer risk. VDR and GC genotypes significantly modify requirements and supplementation response.
clinical_significance: Most common nutritional deficiency globally; below 20 ng/mL indicates deficiency. VDR and GC genotypes modify requirements.

name: Vitamin B12
text_chunk: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is essential for DNA synthesis, methylation, and neurological function. Deficiency below 200 pg/mL causes megaloblastic anemia and irreversible neuropathy. Functional deficiency at 200-400 pg/mL detected by elevated MMA and homocysteine. Causes include pernicious anemia, metformin use, PPI use, and vegan diet.
clinical_significance: Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and irreversible neuropathy; functional deficiency at 200-400 pg/mL requires MMA testing.

name: Folate
text_chunk: Serum folate is essential for one-carbon metabolism, DNA synthesis, and neural tube development. Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia, elevated homocysteine, and neural tube defects. MTHFR C677T impairs conversion to active 5-methyltetrahydrofolate, requiring methylfolate supplementation rather than folic acid.
clinical_significance: Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and neural tube defects; MTHFR genotype determines optimal supplementation form.

name: Vitamin B6
text_chunk: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxal 5-phosphate) is a cofactor in over 100 enzymatic reactions including amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis, and homocysteine metabolism. Low B6 causes elevated homocysteine, peripheral neuropathy, depression, and impaired immunity. Excessive supplementation (>200 mg/day) can cause sensory neuropathy.
clinical_significance: Cofactor in 100+ reactions; deficiency causes neuropathy and elevated homocysteine.

name: Zinc
text_chunk: Zinc is a cofactor for over 300 enzymes, critical for immune function, wound healing, DNA synthesis, and cell division. Deficiency affects 2 billion people globally. Serum zinc is a poor marker of total body status. Zinc is an acute phase reactant that decreases during inflammation.
clinical_significance: Essential for immune function and 300+ enzymes; deficiency impairs wound healing and immunity.

name: Magnesium (RBC)
text_chunk: Red blood cell magnesium is more accurate than serum magnesium for total body status. Magnesium is essential for 600+ enzymatic reactions, ATP production, DNA repair, and neuromuscular function. Deficiency is estimated in 50-80% of the US population and is associated with hypertension, arrhythmias, insulin resistance, and migraines.
clinical_significance: RBC magnesium is more accurate than serum for total body status; deficiency is extremely common and associated with cardiometabolic disease.

name: Selenium
text_chunk: Selenium is incorporated into selenoproteins including glutathione peroxidases and thioredoxin reductases. Critical for thyroid function (deiodinase enzymes require selenium) and immune function. Both deficiency and excess are harmful.
clinical_significance: Essential for thyroid function and antioxidant defense; deficiency impairs T4-to-T3 conversion.

name: Copper
text_chunk: Copper is required for iron metabolism (ceruloplasmin), connective tissue formation (lysyl oxidase), and antioxidant defense (superoxide dismutase). Wilson disease (ATP7B mutations) causes toxic copper accumulation. Excess zinc supplementation can induce copper deficiency.
clinical_significance: Required for iron metabolism; Wilson disease causes toxic accumulation. Zinc supplementation can induce copper deficiency.

name: Omega-3 Index
text_chunk: The Omega-3 Index measures EPA + DHA as a percentage of total RBC fatty acids. Above 8% is cardiovascular protective; below 4% indicates high risk. FADS1 rs174547 significantly affects omega-3 and omega-6 levels by altering desaturase activity. The REDUCE-IT trial showed CV benefit of high-dose EPA.
clinical_significance: Cardiovascular risk marker; above 8% is protective, below 4% high risk. FADS1 genotype modifies omega-3 metabolism.

name: Vitamin A
text_chunk: Vitamin A (retinol) is essential for vision, immune function, reproduction, and cellular differentiation. Deficiency causes night blindness and immune impairment. Toxicity causes hepatotoxicity and teratogenicity. BCMO1 rs12934922 reduces beta-carotene to retinol conversion by 30-60%, meaning carriers require preformed vitamin A.
clinical_significance: Deficiency causes night blindness; toxicity causes liver damage. BCMO1 genotype affects carotene conversion.

name: Vitamin E
text_chunk: Vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol) is the primary lipid-soluble antioxidant. Deficiency is rare but causes ataxia, neuropathy, and hemolytic anemia in fat malabsorption syndromes. Levels should be lipid-adjusted in hyperlipidemia.
clinical_significance: Primary lipid-soluble antioxidant; deficiency causes ataxia and neuropathy in malabsorption.

name: Iodine
text_chunk: Urinary iodine is the primary marker for iodine status. Essential for thyroid hormone synthesis. Deficiency affects 2 billion people globally causing goiter and hypothyroidism. Both deficiency and excess cause thyroid dysfunction.
clinical_significance: Essential for thyroid hormone synthesis; both deficiency and excess cause thyroid dysfunction.

name: DHEA-S
text_chunk: DHEA-S is the most abundant circulating steroid hormone. Peaks in mid-20s and declines ~10% per decade (adrenopause). Low levels associated with aging, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive decline. Used to evaluate adrenal androgen excess and insufficiency. No significant diurnal variation.
clinical_significance: Declines with age; low levels associated with aging and cardiovascular risk.

name: Cortisol
text_chunk: Cortisol is the primary glucocorticoid. Morning cortisol below 3 ug/dL suggests adrenal insufficiency; above 18 excludes it. Cushing syndrome screened with 24h urinary free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, or dexamethasone suppression test. Chronic elevation contributes to visceral obesity and insulin resistance.
clinical_significance: Primary stress hormone; morning cortisol below 3 ug/dL suggests adrenal insufficiency. Chronic elevation causes metabolic syndrome.

name: Testosterone (Total)
text_chunk: Total testosterone is measured in the morning (8-10 AM). Normal adult male range 264-916 ng/dL. Hypogonadism defined as below 300 ng/dL with symptoms. Associated with fatigue, low libido, erectile dysfunction, osteoporosis, and metabolic syndrome. Declines 1-2% per year after age 30.
clinical_significance: Primary male sex hormone; below 300 ng/dL with symptoms defines hypogonadism.

name: Estradiol
text_chunk: Estradiol (E2) is the primary estrogen. In women, varies with menstrual cycle. Low estradiol causes amenorrhea, osteoporosis, and vasomotor symptoms. In men, below 10-20 pg/mL causes osteoporosis; above 40-50 pg/mL causes gynecomastia.
clinical_significance: Primary estrogen; extremes cause bone loss (low) or gynecomastia (high) in men.

name: Progesterone
text_chunk: Progesterone is produced by the corpus luteum after ovulation. Mid-luteal progesterone above 3 ng/mL confirms ovulation; above 10 ng/mL indicates adequate luteal function. Low levels associated with anovulation and recurrent miscarriage.
clinical_significance: Confirms ovulation when above 3 ng/mL; low levels associated with anovulation and recurrent miscarriage.

name: PSA
text_chunk: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) below 4.0 ng/mL is generally normal. PSA velocity, density, and free/total ratio improve specificity. Also elevated in BPH, prostatitis, and after prostate manipulation. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors lower PSA by ~50%.
clinical_significance: Prostate cancer screening marker; PSA velocity and free/total ratio improve specificity.

name: Cystatin C
text_chunk: Cystatin C is a cysteine protease inhibitor produced at constant rate by all nucleated cells. Alternative to creatinine for GFR estimation, unaffected by muscle mass, diet, or sex. GrimAge epigenetic clock component where elevated levels predict accelerated biological aging independent of kidney function.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component; muscle-mass-independent GFR marker.

name: TIMP1
text_chunk: Tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinase 1 (TIMP1) regulates extracellular matrix remodeling. GrimAge epigenetic clock component where elevated levels predict accelerated aging. Associated with fibrosis, cancer progression, and cardiovascular disease.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component; elevated in fibrosis, cancer, and cardiovascular disease.

name: Adrenomedullin
text_chunk: Adrenomedullin (ADM) is a vasodilatory peptide from vascular endothelium and adrenal medulla. GrimAge epigenetic clock component associated with accelerated aging. Biomarker of endothelial dysfunction, sepsis severity, and cardiovascular disease. Predicts mortality in heart failure and sepsis.
clinical_significance: GrimAge clock component and endothelial dysfunction marker; predicts mortality in sepsis and heart failure.

name: NT-proBNP
text_chunk: NT-proBNP is released by cardiomyocytes in response to ventricular wall stress. Primary biomarker for heart failure diagnosis, prognosis, and monitoring. Below 125 pg/mL in non-acute settings excludes HF. Age-specific cutoffs apply. Affected by renal function and obesity.
clinical_significance: Primary heart failure biomarker; below 125 pg/mL excludes chronic HF.

name: Uric Acid
text_chunk: Uric acid is the end product of purine metabolism. Hyperuricemia (>7.0 men, >6.0 women) causes gout. Associated with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, CKD, and metabolic syndrome. Very high levels with chemotherapy indicate tumor lysis syndrome risk.
clinical_significance: Elevated in gout, CKD, and metabolic syndrome; high levels indicate tumor lysis syndrome risk during chemotherapy.

name: Microalbumin/Creatinine Ratio
text_chunk: Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) detects microalbuminuria, the earliest marker of diabetic nephropathy. Normal below 30 mg/g; moderately increased 30-300; severely increased above 300. Annual screening recommended in diabetes and hypertension. ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce albuminuria.
clinical_significance: Earliest marker of diabetic nephropathy; annual screening recommended in diabetes and hypertension.

name: Phosphorus
text_chunk: Serum phosphorus is regulated by kidneys, PTH, and FGF23. Hyperphosphatemia in CKD drives cardiovascular calcification and mortality. Hypophosphatemia in refeeding syndrome is life-threatening. FGF23 is the primary hormonal regulator.
clinical_significance: Hyperphosphatemia in CKD drives cardiovascular calcification; hypophosphatemia in refeeding syndrome is life-threatening.

name: Troponin I
text_chunk: High-sensitivity cardiac troponin I is the gold standard for myocardial injury and acute MI diagnosis. Released within 2-4 hours of injury. Rise/fall pattern with value above 99th percentile required for AMI diagnosis. Serial measurement at 0 and 3 hours enables rapid rule-out.
clinical_significance: Gold standard for acute MI diagnosis; rise/fall pattern required.

name: CK-MB
text_chunk: CK-MB is the cardiac isoform of creatine kinase. Primarily used to detect reinfarction because it clears faster (48-72 hours) than troponin (7-14 days). Less specific than troponin. CK-MB/total CK ratio above 5% suggests cardiac origin.
clinical_significance: Used to detect reinfarction due to faster clearance than troponin.

name: BNP
text_chunk: B-type natriuretic peptide is released in response to ventricular volume overload. Below 100 pg/mL excludes heart failure. Above 400 strongly suggests HF. Sacubitril/valsartan raises BNP but lowers NT-proBNP; use NT-proBNP for monitoring on this drug.
clinical_significance: Heart failure diagnostic marker; below 100 pg/mL excludes HF.

name: PT/INR
text_chunk: Prothrombin time measures extrinsic coagulation pathway, reported as INR. Therapeutic INR for warfarin is 2.0-3.0 (most indications) or 2.5-3.5 (mechanical valves). VKORC1 and CYP2C9 genotypes significantly affect warfarin dose requirements.
clinical_significance: Monitors warfarin therapy; VKORC1/CYP2C9 genotypes guide dosing.

name: Fibrinogen (coag)
text_chunk: Fibrinogen measured functionally in coagulation assessment. Essential for clot formation; consumed in DIC. Below 100 mg/dL requires cryoprecipitate. Critical for viscoelastic testing (TEG/ROTEM) in trauma and surgical bleeding management.
clinical_significance: Essential coagulation factor; below 100 mg/dL in DIC requires cryoprecipitate.

name: D-Dimer
text_chunk: D-Dimer is a fibrin degradation product. Below 500 ng/mL (or age-adjusted cutoff) has high negative predictive value for VTE exclusion. Elevated in VTE, DIC, surgery, trauma, pregnancy, cancer, and infection. Wells score combined with D-Dimer enables safe VTE exclusion without imaging.
clinical_significance: High negative predictive value for VTE exclusion below 500 ng/mL. Age-adjusted cutoffs improve specificity.
